one_line_takeaway: GenTel-Safe pairs an open E5-based, model-agnostic prompt-injection detector (GenTel-Shield) with a large scenario-organized benchmark (GenTel-Bench, 84,812 attacks / 28 scenarios), reaching state-of-the-art detection on jailbreak and goal-hijacking while trailing one baseline on prompt leaking.
key_finding: GenTel-Shield achieves the best overall accuracy and F1 on two of the three attack types: jailbreak (accuracy 97.63%, F1 97.69%) and goal hijacking (accuracy 96.81%, F1 96.74%), beating all 7 baselines. On prompt leaking it is a strong second (F1 97.89%, precision 99.42%, recall 96.42%) but is edged out by WhyLabs LangKit (F1/accuracy 99.34%). Several baselines (Prompt Guard, Deepset, Fmops) reach ~100% recall but only ~50-65% accuracy, i.e. they classify almost everything as an attack, while WhyLabs LangKit does the opposite (high precision, low recall). On the merged out-of-distribution Jailbreak-LLM + Deita test set, GenTel-Shield leads on accuracy (97.26%) and F1 (90.35%). It also maintains near-100% detection in Chinese and Japanese, though ProtectAI surpasses it on French/Spanish/German for goal hijacking.
core_contribution: The paper introduces GenTel-Safe, a unified framework for defending LLMs against prompt injection attacks, comprising two artifacts the authors build themselves: (1) GenTel-Shield, a model-agnostic input-side detection classifier fine-tuned from the multilingual E5 text embedding model that flags a prompt as attack vs. benign without needing access to the protected LLM's internals; and (2) GenTel-Bench, an evaluation benchmark of 84,812 prompt injection attack prompts spanning 3 major attack categories (jailbreaking, goal/target hijacking, prompt leaking) and 28 security scenarios, cross-classified against 10 LLM application scenarios and 6 risk domains. The stated novelty over prior benchmarks is organizing the benchmark by security scenario integrated with application context (rather than only by attack methodology), and organizing the detector's training data with a scenario taxonomy plus semantic and character-level data augmentation.
threat_model: An attacker submits a crafted input prompt to an LLM-integrated application to override safety guardrails. Three attack classes are targeted: jailbreaking (bypass ethical/safety constraints to elicit harmful content, one-step role-play or multi-step dialogue), goal/target hijacking (inject instructions that make the model abandon its intended task and execute the attacker's task), and prompt leaking (extract the confidential system prompt or sensitive data). GenTel-Shield sits in front of the LLM as a decoupled guardrail that classifies incoming prompts and filters malicious ones before they reach the model. The paper does not evaluate adaptive attackers who craft inputs specifically to evade the GenTel-Shield classifier itself.
limitations: Defense is limited to input-side classification of prompt text; it does not cover indirect/retrieval-based injection payloads embedded in tool outputs or documents, nor adaptive attacks tuned against the classifier. GenTel-Shield does not win on prompt leaking (WhyLabs LangKit is better), and its recall drops to 82.68% on the cross-dataset test, showing generalization gaps. No ablation isolates how much the data augmentation vs. the E5 backbone contributes. The benchmark leans heavily on GPT-4o-rewritten templates capped at 60-100 tokens, and multilingual evaluation relies on Google Translate, both of which can introduce distributional artifacts. Reporting quality is uneven: the abstract has typos ('compromises'/'comprises') and Table 3 lists GenTel-Shield's prompt-leaking accuracy as '7.92', an evident transcription error (context implies ~97.92%). Baselines are a mix of open models and commercial APIs (Lakera, WhyLabs), and some were excluded from certain sub-experiments for degenerate behavior, complicating fully apples-to-apples comparison.

datasets_used: GenTel-Bench (own benchmark): 84,812 attack prompts across 3 categories and 28 security scenarios; full set 1:1 balanced, >170,000 data points, 10 application scenarios
Jailbreak-LLM (Shen et al., 2024): 1,405 jailbreak attack prompts
Deita (Liu et al., 2023a): 9,500 benign instruction entries
Training sources (disjoint from benchmark): jailbreakchat.com, reddit.com, VMware Open-Instruct, Chatbot Instruction Prompts (alespalla)
quantitative_results: Jailbreak attack (Table 1) GenTel-Shield: accuracy 97.63%, precision 98.04%, F1 97.69%, recall 97.34% (best overall)
Goal hijacking (Table 2) GenTel-Shield: accuracy 96.81%, precision 99.44%, F1 96.74%, recall 94.19% (best overall)
Prompt leaking (Table 3) GenTel-Shield: precision 99.42%, F1 97.89%, recall 96.42% (accuracy misprinted as 7.92, ~97.92); WhyLabs LangKit higher at accuracy/F1 99.34%, precision 99.62%
Cross-dataset Jailbreak-LLM + Deita (Table 4) GenTel-Shield: accuracy 97.26%, precision 99.57%, F1 90.35%, recall 82.68% (best accuracy and F1)
Multilingual: near-100% jailbreak detection in Chinese/Japanese/French/Spanish; peak prompt-leaking accuracy 97.36% in Chinese; ProtectAI best on French/Spanish/German for goal hijacking
Per-risk accuracy ~0.99 for GenTel-Shield on Violence (R1), Privacy Violation (R4), Cybercrime (R11)
